title: The Flowering Rod
subtitle: How Mary, vowed to God, was betrothed to Joseph when his rod alone burst into bloom

stillness: Be still before the mystery of her hidden years.
She grew daily in virtue, daily visited by the angels.
Let your soul grow quiet, and watch.
gospel: There shall come forth a rod out of the root of Jesse,
and a flower shall rise up out of his root.
Moses found that the rod of Aaron had budded,
and the swelling buds had broken into flowers,
which, their leaves opening, were formed into almonds.
behold: The Virgin of the Lord, Mary, grew daily in virtue;
and because her father and mother had forsaken her, the Lord took her up.
Daily she was visited by the angels, daily she enjoyed the divine visitation,
which kept her from every evil and made her overflow with every good;
and thus she came to her fourteenth year.
Then the High Priest decreed that the virgins kept in the temple, having reached this age,
should return home and give themselves to marriage.
The rest obeyed; but the Virgin Mary alone answered that this she could not do:
for her parents had given her over to the service of the Lord,
and she herself had vowed her virginity to God.
And the High Priest was set in great distress (in angustia positus):
he dared neither break the Scripture that says, 'Vow, and pay your vows,'
nor bring in a custom unknown to the people.
So he took counsel, and all agreed the Lord must be consulted.
And while the others bent to prayer, a voice came from the place of the mercy-seat:
by Isaiah's prophecy it must be sought to whom the Virgin should be betrothed —
'There shall come forth a rod out of the root of Jesse.'
He commanded every unmarried man of the house and family of David to bring his rod to the altar.
Among them was a man named Joseph;
and when he had brought his rod, at once it put forth a flower,
and a dove, coming down from heaven, settled upon its top —
and it was made plain to all that to him the Virgin should be betrothed.
She returned then to Nazareth, never going forth without the guardians of her modesty;
for, as Jerome says, modesty must be inseparable from virginity,
and without it true chastity cannot be kept.
consider: By this rod is understood the blessed Virgin Mary:
slender, like the rod, through her poverty;
supple through her humility;
upright through her intention and her charity.
This Virgin was found with swelling buds when she conceived the Son of God in her womb;
and from her fruitful flowers broke forth, when the Son of God was born, she remaining a virgin.
For as the flower does not violate the tree, but adorns it,
so the Son of God did not violate the Virgin, but the more adorned her with gifts and graces.
This is the rod which, set in the tabernacle of testimony,
without the moisture of the earth brought forth the fruit of the nut;
for without the seed of man she brought forth the Son,
who, like a nut, clung to the wood of the passion.
And rightly is He called now flower, now fruit:
flower in the prophetic promise of the Old Testament,
fruit in the perfection of grace in the New.
But as the fruit does not appear while the flower is green,
so the truth of Christ was not made plain while the carnal observance remained;
but as the flower withers, the fruit is seen —
for, the Law falling away, grace and truth are held to be made by Jesus Christ.
And concerning this betrothal Anselm says:
two things she more gladly loved — virginity and fruitfulness:
virginity, for she understood it to please God above all things;
fruitfulness, for without it she feared to incur the curse of the Law, still kept after the flesh.
Two things, then, were in the Virgin's soul, contending in a great unseen battle:
the love of virginity, and the fear of the Law's curse.
Yet, the matter long and deeply weighed, love conquered, which weighed the heavier,
and fear gave its back and fled.
prayer: Lord Jesus, flower out of the root of Jesse,
You did not violate the Virgin, but adorned her the more with gifts and graces.
You are the flower in the promise, the fruit in grace and truth;
the nut that clung to the wood of the passion.
As the flower withers and the fruit is seen,
so let the carnal observance fall away in me,
and let grace and truth, made by You, be held fast.
In Your love let fear give its back and flee.

latinJewel.latin: Nam sicut flos non violat arborem, sed ornat; sic Dei Filius non violavit Virginem, sed magis donis et gratiis adornavit.
latinJewel.english: For as the flower does not violate the tree, but adorns it, so the Son of God did not violate the Virgin, but the more adorned her with gifts and graces.
